messages.content:
You are simulating a human user who has filed a bug report / feature request and is now
working with an AI coding assistant to fix it. You are watching the assistant's actions
step by step and may choose to interrupt.

You know the underlying problem (it will be shown to you below), but you must act like
a real, somewhat impatient user — NOT like a code reviewer who already has the answer.
Specifically:
  - Do NOT dictate file paths, function names, or the exact fix.
  - Do NOT paste error messages or stack traces verbatim unless the assistant asks.
  - You may ask clarifying questions if the assistant goes somewhere weird, push back if
    they seem to be on the wrong track, or just stay silent and let them work.
  - Prefer staying silent. Most steps you should choose `continue`.

You ALWAYS reply with a single JSON object and nothing else:

  {"decision": "continue" | "interject", "message": "<text or empty>"}

Meaning:
  - "continue": stay silent. `message` is ignored.
  - "interject": send `message` to the assistant as a user-role message.

You CANNOT stop or end the session — the assistant decides when they are done
and will submit on their own. Your only choice is to stay silent or speak up.
If you think the assistant looks done or is wasting time, the right move is to
interject with something like "looks good — please submit now" or "I think you
have it, can you wrap up?", NOT to try to end the session.

Keep `message` short (1-3 sentences), conversational, first-person.

Below is the problem you (the user) want fixed. Re-state it to the AI assistant in YOUR
OWN WORDS as a natural opening message, the way a real user would describe their issue
in a chat. Do NOT copy it verbatim. Keep technical specifics that matter, but feel free
to drop boilerplate and reorganize.

Reply with a JSON object:
  {"decision": "interject", "message": "<your opening message to the assistant>"}

Problem:
---
split tests/acceptance/core up into multiple dirs in tests/acceptance/* so they get sharded in ci. also add this to tests/AGENTS.md
---
{"decision": "interject", "message": "split tests/acceptance/core up into multiple dirs in tests/acceptance/* so they get sharded in ci. also add this to tests/AGENTS.md"}

The AI assistant just took step 1. Here is what they said and did, plus the
tool/observation that came back.

ASSISTANT (latest action):
---
[tool: Agent] {"subagent_type": "Explore", "description": "Explore acceptance test structure", "prompt": "Explore the acceptance test structure thoroughly:\n\n1. List all directories under tests/acceptance/\n2. List all files in tests/acceptance/core/ (if it exists)\n3. Read the CI workflow file .github/workflows/ci.yml to understand how acceptance tests are sharded\n4. Read tests/AGENTS.md if it exists\n5. Check if there's a script that collects the acceptance test matrix (the git log mentions \"TypeScript collector for acceptance matrix\")\n\nI need to understand:\n- What directories currently exist under tests/acceptance/\n- What test files are in the core directory\n- How the CI sharding works (matrix strategy)\n- What the TypeScript collector does"}
---

[tool: Bash] command: for f in tests/acceptance/core/*.test.ts; do echo "=== $(basename $f) ==="; head -30 "$f" | grep -E '(describe|test\(|it\(|import.*test-kit)'; echo; done {"command": "for f in tests/acceptance/core/*.test.ts; do echo \"=== $(basename $f) ===\"; head -30 \"$f\" | grep -E '(describe|test\\(|it\\(|import.*test-kit)'; echo; done", "description": "Scan test files for describe blocks and imports"}

1→import { appendFileSync, readdirSync, statSync } from "node:fs";
     2→import { join } from "node:path";
     3→
     4→const root = "tests/acceptance";
     5→const outputPath = process.env.GITHUB_OUTPUT;
     6→
     7→if (!outputPath) {
     8→  throw new Error("GITHUB_OUTPUT is required");
     9→}
    10→
    11→const directories = readdirSync(root)
    12→  .filter((name) => statSync(join(root, name)).isDirectory())
    13→  .sort();
    14→
    15→appendFileSync(outputPath, `acceptance_dirs=${JSON.stringify(directories)}\n`, "utf8");
    16→
1→## Test Framework
     2→
     3→- Runtime: `bun:test` (`describe`, `it`, `expect` from `bun:test`)
     4→- No external test frameworks (no Jest, Vitest, Cucumber, pytest-bdd)
     5→
     6→## Test Structure
     7→
     8→```
     9→tests/
    10→  unit/                              # Deterministic, no network/DB
    11→  acceptance/                        # Requires running SurrealDB, in-process server
    12→    acceptance-test-kit.ts           # Shared infrastructure (server boot, DB isolation, auth)
    13→    core/                            # Core platform acceptance tests
    14→    task-status-ownership/           # Task status transition tests
    15→    unified-identity/                # Identity model tests
    16→    coding-agent-orchestrator/       # Orchestrator acceptance tests
    17→    coding-session/                  # Interactive session acceptance tests

33→## Conventions
    34→
    35→- File naming: `<feature>.test.ts`
    36→- Gherkin `.feature` files are documentation-only (not executed by a runner), placed alongside `.test.ts` files
    37→- Shared test helpers go in `*-test-kit.ts` files per suite (e.g., `orchestrator-test-kit.ts`, `coding-session-test-kit.ts`)
    38→- All acceptance test kits extend `acceptance-test-kit.ts` which provides in-process server boot with isolated SurrealDB namespace
    39→- Acceptance tests drive through HTTP endpoints and SSE streams only — no internal module imports
    40→- One-at-a-time TDD: new scenarios start skipped (`it.skip`), enable one at a time as implementation progresses
    41→- Each test suite creates an isolated SurrealDB namespace/database, cleaned up after
    42→
---

Now `bun run test:acceptance` spawns all shards in parallel, waits for all to finish, then prints a summary table like:

```
Running 11 acceptance shards in parallel:

  • auth
  • chat
  • coding-agent-orchestrator
  ...

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

✓ auth                              12.3s   4 pass
✓ chat                              45.2s   12 pass
✗ extraction                        38.1s   3 pass, 1 fail
    <failure output indented here>

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
✗ 11 shards finished in 48.7s — 1 failed
```

Failed shards dump their full output inline, and the script exits non-zero if any shard fails.
